steps.title: Verify the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what part of the control system is not responding. Check whether the issue affects: Touchscreen only, Physical buttons only, Both touchscreen and physical controls, Monitor cart controls, C-arm stand controls, Footswitch operation, System boot or login screen. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to a touchscreen, control panel, cart, stand, or system-wide issue. If only one control area is affected, continue troubleshooting that area. If the entire system is frozen, proceed as a system control failure.

steps.title: Check for an Active Procedure Lock, Mode Selection, or User Prompt
steps.instructions: Look for any message, confirmation box, safety prompt, login screen, or mode selection that may prevent normal control. Review the touchscreen and monitor display for prompts requiring confirmation. Check whether the system is waiting for a user login, exam selection, patient selection, or procedure mode. Confirm whether fluoroscopy, image review, or system movement is disabled by a selected mode or workflow step. Expected outcome: If the system was waiting for a required selection, complete the prompt and verify control returns. If this resolves the issue, document the user interface condition and return the unit to service.

steps.title: Inspect the Touchscreen Surface
steps.instructions: Check for anything interfering with touch recognition. Inspect the screen for fluid, gel, cleaning residue, tape, drape material, cracked glass, heavy scratches, or pressure from attached accessories. Clean the touchscreen using the facility-approved method. Remove anything touching the screen edge or resting against the display. Expected outcome: The touchscreen responds normally after obstruction or contamination is removed. If this resolves the issue, stop troubleshooting and document the external cause.

steps.title: Check for Glove or Stylus Interaction Issues
steps.instructions: Confirm whether the screen responds differently depending on how it is touched. Have a trained operator test the screen with a clean dry finger if permitted by infection control policy. Check whether gloves are wet, heavily powdered, double-layered, or contaminated with gel or fluids. Do not use sharp objects, pens, tools, or metal probes on the touchscreen. Expected outcome: If the touchscreen responds with proper contact, the issue may be user interaction or contamination related. If the screen remains unresponsive, continue troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify Physical Control Buttons and Emergency Stop Status
steps.instructions: Check whether physical system controls are functional. Test available non-imaging controls only when safe. Check whether the emergency stop is activated. Confirm that any emergency stop button is fully released according to normal user operation. Inspect control buttons for sticking, damage, missing caps, or fluid intrusion. Expected outcome: Physical controls should respond normally unless the system is locked, frozen, or has a control hardware problem. If emergency stop was active and releasing it restores control, verify safe operation before returning the unit to service.

steps.title: Check Footswitch and Hand Control Accessories
steps.instructions: A connected accessory can sometimes appear like a control failure if it is damaged, stuck, disconnected, or not recognized. Inspect the footswitch cable and connector for damage, bent pins, looseness, or contamination. Confirm the footswitch is connected to the correct port. Check whether any footswitch pedal appears stuck or physically damaged. If a known-good compatible footswitch is available, swap it only if allowed by facility process. Expected outcome: The system recognizes control accessories and no stuck input is present. If a defective accessory is found, remove it from service and test the C-arm with a known-good accessory.

steps.title: Check Cables Between the C-Arm Stand and Monitor Cart
steps.instructions: A communication issue between the imaging stand and monitor cart may cause missing controls, frozen screens, or partial system response. Inspect external interconnect cables between the stand and monitor cart. Confirm connectors are fully seated and latched. Look for crushed cables, stretched cables, bent pins, or strain at connector ends. Avoid forcing connectors. Expected outcome: External communication cables are secure and undamaged. If reseating a loose cable restores normal control, perform a functional check before returning the system to service.

steps.title: Confirm Power Stability
steps.instructions: Touchscreen and system control failures can occur if the system has unstable power or incomplete startup. Verify the C-arm is connected to a known-good hospital-grade outlet. Check that the power cord is fully seated and undamaged. Confirm there are no loose plugs, damaged outlet faces, tripped breakers, or power strips being used improperly. If safe and appropriate, test the outlet or use a verified outlet. Expected outcome: The system has stable AC power and is not affected by an external power issue. If power instability is found, correct the power source issue and retest.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled Restart If Safe
steps.instructions: If the system is frozen or the touchscreen is locked up, a controlled restart may clear a temporary software state. Confirm the C-arm is not needed for an active procedure. Notify clinical staff before restarting. Use the normal shutdown process if the controls allow it. If the system is completely frozen, follow facility-approved power cycling practice. Allow the system to fully shut down before restarting. Expected outcome: The system restarts normally, the touchscreen responds, and controls function after boot. If the issue clears after restart, perform a basic operational check before returning the unit to service.

steps.title: Check for Repeated Freezing or Delayed Response
steps.instructions: A one-time software freeze may clear, but repeated failures should not be ignored. Observe whether the touchscreen responds slowly, intermittently, or only after long delays. Check whether the failure occurs during startup, exam selection, image acquisition, image review, or shutdown. Ask staff whether the issue happens after the unit has been on for a long time. Note any error messages, system warnings, or abnormal sounds. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is documented clearly. If the issue repeats, remove the C-arm from service for further evaluation.

steps.title: Perform a Basic Functional Check
steps.instructions: Before returning the C-arm to service, verify that normal controls are working. Confirm touchscreen response across multiple areas of the screen. Verify physical controls respond appropriately. Confirm the monitor cart and C-arm stand communicate normally. Check system startup, shutdown, exam workflow, image review controls, and basic movement controls where safe. Do not perform radiation testing unless authorized and conditions are appropriate. Expected outcome: The system controls are responsive, stable, and safe for clinical use. If any control remains unreliable, do not return the unit to service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Siemens Healthineers Cios Alpha touchscreen was not responding and system controls were intermittently frozen during setup.
CCR.Cause: External inspection found no active procedure, no screen obstruction, stable AC power, and secure stand-to-cart connections, but the control failure returned after restart.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the C-arm from service, labeled it Out of Service, documented the repeated touchscreen/control failure, and escalated the unit for repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether monitor cart controls or stand controls failed
Cable condition between stand and cart
Whether failure was intermittent or constant

title: Siemens Healthineers Cios Alpha C-Arm - DICOM/PACS Export Failure
description: Troubleshooting Cios Alpha DICOM or PACS export failures caused by network, destination, patient data, or configuration issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Before troubleshooting, confirm the C-arm is not actively needed for a procedure. Do not troubleshoot DICOM export during an active case unless clinical staff confirms it is safe. Confirm images needed for patient care are still visible locally on the Cios Alpha. If imaging is urgently needed, allow the clinical team to continue using the system locally while export troubleshooting waits. Do not delete studies, exams, or patient data during troubleshooting. Expected outcome: Patient care continues safely, and stored images are protected.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Export Failure
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what is failing. Ask whether the issue affects one study, one patient, one destination, or all exports. Check whether the export fails immediately, stays queued, times out, or reports a specific DICOM/network error. Confirm whether images are stored locally on the C-arm. Try exporting a non-critical test or completed study if permitted by site policy. Expected outcome: You identify whether this is a single-study issue, destination issue, or system-wide export problem. If export works for other studies or destinations, stop broad troubleshooting and focus on the affected study, patient data, or destination.

steps.title: Confirm the C-Arm Is Connected to the Network
steps.instructions: Check the simplest external network causes first. Verify the network cable is connected securely to the Cios Alpha or docking/network connection point. Confirm the cable is connected to the correct wall jack or network switch port. Look for link/activity lights at the network jack, switch, or adapter if visible. If the system uses a docking station, cart interface, or hospital network drop, reseat the connection. Expected outcome: The C-arm has a valid physical network connection. If a loose or disconnected cable is found, reconnect it and retry export. If the export succeeds, document the cable/network connection issue and stop.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Network Drop or Cable
steps.instructions: Rule out a bad cable, jack, or local network path. Swap the Ethernet cable with a known-good cable if available. Move to a known-working network jack approved for imaging/PACS traffic if practical. Avoid using random public or non-clinical network ports unless IT confirms they are configured correctly. Confirm whether other imaging devices on the same network area are exporting normally. Expected outcome: The export either works on a known-good connection or continues to fail. If the export works after changing the cable or network drop, the original cable, jack, or network path is the likely cause. Stop and document the finding.

steps.title: Check for Local Network Address Problems
steps.instructions: Confirm the system appears to have normal network connectivity. Review the C-arm network status/settings if accessible under normal user or service-permitted menus. Look for missing IP address, incorrect subnet, gateway issue, or disconnected network status. Confirm whether the device is configured for DHCP or static IP according to site standards. Compare the displayed network information to the hospital’s expected configuration, if available. Expected outcome: The C-arm has a valid network identity for the imaging network. If the IP address is missing, incorrect, duplicated, or outside the expected range, escalate to IT/PACS support or approved service support before changing network configuration.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct DICOM/PACS Destination Is Selected
steps.instructions: A wrong destination can look like a device failure. Verify the selected export destination is the correct PACS, archive, workstation, or DICOM node. Confirm the destination name matches the site workflow. If multiple destinations exist, try a known-good destination if permitted. Ask staff whether a recent PACS, network, or destination change occurred. Expected outcome: The system is attempting export to the correct destination. If another DICOM destination works, the issue is likely destination-specific rather than a total C-arm failure.

steps.title: Verify Patient and Study Information
steps.instructions: Incorrect or incomplete patient/study data can interfere with DICOM workflow. Confirm the patient name, patient ID/MRN, accession number, study date, and modality information are entered correctly. Check for unusual characters, missing required fields, duplicate patient entries, or mismatched accession numbers. Confirm whether the study was created through the expected worklist process or manually entered. If the problem is limited to one patient or exam, compare it with a study that exported successfully. Expected outcome: Required DICOM fields are complete and reasonable. If correcting patient/study information allows export, document the data issue and stop.

steps.title: Check Modality Worklist or Order Association
steps.instructions: If the site uses modality worklist, export may depend on proper order selection. Confirm the exam was selected from the correct worklist entry when applicable. Verify the accession number and patient identifiers match the order in the hospital system. Ask clinical staff whether the case was performed under an emergency/manual entry workflow. If the worklist is not loading or patient data does not match, involve PACS/RIS/IT support. Expected outcome: The exported study is properly associated with the correct order. If the study exports after proper order association, the issue was likely workflow or demographic mismatch.

steps.title: Review the Export Queue
steps.instructions: Check whether studies are stuck, pending, failed, or repeatedly retrying. Open the export, send, archive, or network queue if available. Look for failed studies, repeated retries, or a backlog of unsent exams. Confirm whether all studies are stuck or only one study is failing. Do not clear, delete, or purge queued studies unless approved by clinical leadership and site policy. Expected outcome: You determine whether the problem is a queue backlog, a single failed study, or total DICOM communication failure. If one study is blocking the queue, escalate to PACS/IT or vendor support before deleting or altering data.

steps.title: Confirm PACS or DICOM Destination Availability
steps.instructions: The C-arm may be functioning normally while the receiving system is unavailable. Ask PACS/IT whether the destination is online and accepting DICOM studies. Confirm whether other modalities are sending successfully to the same destination. Verify whether there was recent PACS maintenance, network maintenance, firewall change, or server migration. Check whether the destination AE Title, IP address, and port may have changed. Expected outcome: The receiving PACS/DICOM node is confirmed available. If other devices also cannot export, the issue is likely PACS, network, or destination-side rather than the Cios Alpha.

steps.title: Check for Recent Configuration Changes
steps.instructions: Determine whether the issue started after a change. Ask staff when export last worked. Check whether the C-arm was moved to a different room, network jack, or OR. Ask whether the hospital changed PACS, VLANs, firewall rules, IP ranges, AE Titles, or ports. Review recent service activity, software updates, or network configuration changes. Expected outcome: You identify whether the failure began after a known environmental or configuration change. If the issue began after a network or PACS change, coordinate with IT/PACS and vendor support as needed.

steps.title: Power Cycle Only When Safe
steps.instructions: A restart may clear a temporary software or communication hang, but it should not interrupt care. Confirm the C-arm is not being used for an active procedure. Verify images are stored locally before restarting. Perform a normal shutdown and restart according to site procedure. After restart, confirm the system boots normally and retry export. Expected outcome: Temporary software or network session issues may clear. If export succeeds after restart, monitor for recurrence and document the restart as the corrective action.

steps.title: Escalate for DICOM Configuration or Internal Fault Evaluation
steps.instructions: Do not guess or make unauthorized DICOM configuration changes. Escalate to PACS/IT if network reachability, AE Title, IP address, port, firewall, worklist, or destination availability is suspected. Escalate to Siemens service or approved imaging service support if the C-arm appears to have a software, storage, DICOM service, or internal communication issue. Preserve failed export details, error messages, timestamps, patient/study identifiers, and destination name for support. Expected outcome: The correct support team receives enough information to isolate the failure.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported that the Siemens Healthineers Cios Alpha C-arm would not export images to PACS after a completed case.
CCR.Cause: Troubleshooting found the C-arm connected to a network wall jack that was not configured for the imaging/PACS network.
CCR.Resolution: Reconnected the C-arm to an approved imaging network port, verified local image storage, successfully exported a test study to PACS, and returned the unit to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue affected one study or all studies
Date and time of failed export
Accessories swapped, including Ethernet cable, docking connection, or network adapter
Power behavior before and after restart
Environmental factors, including room, network jack, OR, or recent relocation
Network jack or room location used
Whether PACS/IT or Siemens service was contacted

title: Siemens Healthineers Cios Alpha C-Arm - Cooling / Thermal Warning During Use
description: Troubleshooting cooling or thermal warnings during C-arm use caused by heat buildup, airflow restriction, workload, or environmental conditions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Before troubleshooting, confirm the procedure can continue safely. Confirm with the clinical team whether imaging is urgently needed. If the warning affects imaging availability, dose delivery, or system operation, have another imaging option available if possible. Do not power cycle, move, or service the C-arm while it is actively needed for patient care unless the clinical team confirms it is safe. Expected outcome: Patient care is protected before the thermal issue is investigated. Why it matters: A cooling or thermal warning may progress to limited imaging, automatic shutdown, or interrupted fluoroscopy.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Warning
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact warning message displayed on the Cios Alpha. Check whether the warning appears on the main system display, monitor cart, generator interface, or system status area. Ask staff when the warning appeared: During continuous fluoro. After repeated exposures. During startup. After the unit was moved. After being stored in a warm area. Expected outcome: The thermal warning is confirmed and the operating conditions are understood. If the message clears and does not return after normal cool-down, document the event and return the unit to service if all functions pass.

steps.title: Stop Imaging Long Enough to Allow Cooling
steps.instructions: Pause imaging if clinically acceptable. Ask staff to stop fluoroscopy or exposure use while keeping the system powered as appropriate. Allow the system time to cool with normal fans operating. Observe whether the warning changes, clears, or escalates. Expected outcome: A workload-related temperature warning should improve after reduced use. If the warning clears and imaging resumes normally, the issue was likely related to heat buildup from heavy use. Document and stop.

steps.title: Check for Blocked Airflow
steps.instructions: Inspect the C-arm and monitor cart for blocked vents or restricted airflow. Look for drapes, covers, linens, bags, wall clearance issues, or equipment carts blocking ventilation openings. Make sure the system is not pushed tightly against a wall or cabinet. Verify that the system has enough space around cooling vents. Expected outcome: Airflow paths are open and unrestricted. If blocked airflow is found, remove the obstruction, allow the system to cool, and retest operation.

steps.title: Check Room Conditions
steps.instructions: Evaluate whether the room environment may be contributing to overheating. Check if the room is unusually warm. Confirm the unit is not positioned near warming devices, direct sunlight, heat exhaust, or other equipment generating heat. Ask whether the system was recently moved from a hot storage area into the procedure room. Expected outcome: The system is operating in a reasonable clinical environment with no obvious external heat source. If environmental heat appears to be the cause, allow the system to stabilize in the room before returning it to active use.

steps.title: Listen for Cooling Fan Operation
steps.instructions: Without opening covers, listen for normal fan operation. Stand near the system base, generator area, and monitor cart. Listen for normal fan noise. Check for unusual sounds such as grinding, rattling, intermittent fan cycling, or no fan operation when the unit is warm. Expected outcome: Cooling fans sound normal and consistent. If fan noise is abnormal, absent, or unusually loud, remove the device from service for repair evaluation.

steps.title: Check for Dust or External Contamination
steps.instructions: Inspect accessible exterior vents and filters, if externally accessible. Look for dust buildup, lint, plastic wrap, tape, or debris on exterior vent areas. Do not remove internal covers unless permitted by your facility procedure and service documentation. Clean only external surfaces using approved methods. Expected outcome: Exterior cooling paths are visibly clear. If vents are heavily contaminated or the warning returns after cleaning, remove the unit from service for bench or vendor evaluation.

steps.title: Review Use Pattern With Clinical Staff
steps.instructions: Determine whether the warning occurred during unusually demanding imaging use. Ask whether the case involved long fluoro time, repeated high-dose imaging, large patient body habitus, steep angles, or extended continuous operation. Check whether staff noticed the warning after a specific exposure pattern. Expected outcome: You can determine whether the warning is likely due to normal thermal protection during heavy use. If the unit only warns after unusually heavy use and recovers normally, document the workload condition and educate staff to allow cooling breaks when clinically possible.

steps.title: Check System Startup and Self-Test Behavior
steps.instructions: After the system has cooled, restart only if clinically safe and allowed by facility procedure. Power the system down normally. Wait briefly before restarting. Observe startup behavior, self-test messages, fan behavior, and whether the thermal warning returns immediately. Expected outcome: A heat-related warning should not return immediately after proper cool-down. If the thermal warning appears immediately at startup while the system is cool, suspect a temperature sensor, cooling control, fan, generator, power, or internal electronics issue.

steps.title: Perform Basic Functional Verification
steps.instructions: Once the warning clears, perform a limited operational check according to site policy. Confirm the system powers normally. Verify monitor display and image chain operation. Confirm C-arm movement is normal. Check that no cooling, temperature, generator, or system error returns. Perform a safe test exposure only if permitted and appropriate. Expected outcome: The system operates normally without recurring thermal warning. If the warning returns quickly under normal use, remove the device from service.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported a cooling/thermal warning on the Siemens Cios Alpha during fluoroscopy use.
CCR.Cause: Found the warning occurred after extended imaging use with restricted airflow near the system vents.
CCR.Resolution: Cleared airflow obstruction, allowed system to cool, verified normal startup and imaging function, and returned the C-arm to service.

helpfulDetails: Room temperature or unusual heat conditions
Approximate fluoro time before warning appeared
Whether warning occurred during continuous or repeated imaging
Any unusual smell, heat, or noise
Whether the warning cleared after cool-down
Whether warning returned immediately at startup
